questions.question: Explain the difference between static routing and dynamic routing. Also list two advantages and two disadvantages of each.
keyPoints: Static routing: manual configuration, fixed routes
Dynamic routing: automatic adjustment using protocols
Static advantages: predictable, low overhead, secure
Static disadvantages: no adaptability, manual intervention needed
Dynamic advantages: auto-adaptation, scales well
Dynamic disadvantages: complex, bandwidth overhead

questions.question: What is a routing metric? Explain any three commonly used routing metrics with examples.
keyPoints: Routing metric is a value used to determine best path
Hop count: number of routers (used in RIP)
Bandwidth: capacity of link (used in OSPF)
Delay: time taken for packet travel
Cost: based on bandwidth (used in OSPF)
Reliability: link failure probability

questions.question: Describe the working principle of Distance Vector routing algorithm with a suitable example.
keyPoints: Each router maintains distance to all destinations
Routers exchange tables with neighbors periodically
Uses Bellman-Ford algorithm for shortest path
Example with 3-4 routers showing table updates
Count to infinity problem explanation

questions.question: What is meant by route convergence? Why is it important in dynamic routing?
keyPoints: Convergence: all routers have consistent routing information
After topology change, routers exchange information
All routers agree on network topology
Important for correct packet delivery
Faster convergence = less disruption
Split horizon and triggered updates help convergence

questions.question: Explain Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm used in link-state routing protocols.
keyPoints: Algorithm finds shortest path from source to all nodes
Uses greedy approach with priority queue
Initialize source distance = 0, others = infinity
Select node with minimum distance, update neighbors
Mark visited nodes, repeat until all reached
Time complexity: O(E log V) with heap

questions.question: Compare Distance Vector and Link State routing protocols under the following heads: (a) Information shared (b) Convergence time (c) Scalability (d) Loop prevention.
keyPoints: (a) DV: distance tables with neighbors; LS: link-state to all routers
(b) DV: slower convergence; LS: faster convergence
(c) DV: limited scalability; LS: better scalability
(d) DV: split horizon, poison reverse; LS: less prone to loops

questions.question: What is Administrative Distance? How does a router use it when multiple routing protocols are configured?
keyPoints: AD is trustworthiness measure of routing information
Lower AD = more trusted route
Common AD values: Connected=0, Static=1, RIP=120, OSPF=110
When multiple protocols exist, router picks route with lowest AD
Enables route prioritization in multi-protocol environments

questions.question: Explain the Count-to-Infinity problem in Distance Vector routing. How does Split Horizon with Poison Reverse solve this problem?
keyPoints: Count-to-Infinity: routing loop causes hop count to increase indefinitely
Example with two routers A and B, link failure
Routers keep incrementing metric until infinity reached
Split Horizon: don't advertise routes back to source interface
Poison Reverse: advertise route with infinite metric back to source
Prevents false updates and routing loops

questions.question: With a sample network topology of 5 nodes, demonstrate how Dijkstra's algorithm calculates the shortest path from source to all other nodes. Show step-by-step procedure.
keyPoints: Define network with 5 nodes and weighted edges
Initialize: source=0, others=infinity, visited set=empty
Step 1: select minimum unvisited node, mark visited
Step 2: update neighbor distances if shorter found
Step 3: repeat until all nodes visited
Show final shortest path table with distances
Explain path extraction for each destination

questions.question: Compare RIP (Routing Information Protocol) and OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) routing protocols in detail, covering: Protocol type, Metric used, Convergence mechanism, Administrative Distance, Advantages and Limitations.
keyPoints: RIP: Distance Vector, OSPF: Link State
RIP: hop count (max 15), OSPF: cost based on bandwidth
RIP: periodic updates every 30s, OSPF: triggered updates on change
RIP: AD=120, OSPF: AD=110
RIP: simple, limited scalability, max 15 hops
OSPF: hierarchical, fast convergence, more complex
RIP uses UDP 520, OSPF uses IP protocol 89
RIP，OSPF

questions.question: Explain the three phases of Link State routing: (i) Discovery (ii) Building Link State Packets (iii) Flooding and (iv) Route calculation. Also explain why Link State converges faster than Distance Vector.
keyPoints: Discovery: routers discover neighbors and their costs
Building LSP: router creates packet with local links
Flooding: LSP flooded to all routers in area
Route calculation: each router runs Dijkstra on collected LSPs
LS maintains complete topology map
LS floods only changes, not periodic full updates
LS has faster convergence due to complete information
DV relies on iterative process causing slower convergence
